eli3: Matrix decompositions factor matrices into simpler forms, revealing structure and enabling efficient computation. Think of factoring numbers: 12 = 3 √ó 4. For matrices: A = product of simpler matrices with nice properties.

Common decompositions: LU (for solving systems), QR (for least squares, orthogonalization), SVD (for everything ‚Äî low-rank approximation, PCA, pseudo-inverse), Eigendecomposition (for powers, exponentials, dynamics).

Choosing the right decomposition depends on the problem: symmetric positive definite ‚Üí Cholesky; general dense ‚Üí LU or QR; rank-deficient/ill-conditioned ‚Üí SVD; large sparse ‚Üí iterative methods (GMRES, Conjugate Gradient).

whenWhy.title: Solving linear systems
whenWhy.body: LU: Ax = b becomes LUx = b, solve Ly = b then Ux = y. O(n¬≥) upfront, O(n¬≤) per RHS. Cholesky for SPD: A = LL·µÄ, half the cost. Once factored, multiple RHS are cheap.

whenWhy.title: Least squares problems
whenWhy.body: QR: A = QR, solve R x = Q·µÄ b. More stable than normal equations (A·µÄA)‚Åª¬πA·µÄb which squares condition number. SVD: A = UŒ£V·µÄ, x = VŒ£‚Å∫U·µÄb gives minimum norm solution for rank-deficient A.

whenWhy.title: PCA and dimensionality reduction
whenWhy.body: SVD of data matrix X. Right singular vectors V are principal components. Œ£¬≤ gives eigenvalues of covariance. Truncated SVD for low-rank approximation. Eigendecomposition of covariance X·µÄX = VŒõV·µÄ equivalent but less stable.

whenWhy.title: Matrix functions and differential equations
whenWhy.body: Eigendecomposition A = VDV‚Åª¬π: A‚Åø = VD‚ÅøV‚Åª¬π, e^A = Ve^DV‚Åª¬π. For solving ·∫ã = Ax. Schur decomposition for non-diagonalizable matrices. Matrix square roots, logarithms via eigendecomposition or SVD.

deepDiveIntro: LU decomposition: A = PLU where P is permutation, L is unit lower triangular, U is upper triangular. Cholesky: A = LL·µÄ for SPD A. QR: A = QR where Q orthogonal, R upper triangular. SVD: A = UŒ£V·µÄ. Eigendecomposition: A = VDV‚Åª¬π for diagonalizable A. Schur: A = UTU* where U unitary, T upper triangular.

Complexity: LU and Cholesky O(n¬≥), QR O(mn¬≤) for m√ón with m‚â•n, SVD O(mn¬≤). For large sparse matrices, use iterative methods (Conjugate Gradient for SPD, GMRES for general) or sparse direct methods (supernodal, multifrontal).

deepDive.heading: LU Decomposition
deepDive.body: A = PLU. P for pivoting (stability). L unit lower triangular (1s on diagonal), U upper triangular. Solve Ax=b: Ly=Pb (forward substitution), Ux=y (back substitution). Cholesky A=LL·µÄ for SPD: half storage, half operations, no pivoting needed, always stable.
deepDive.formula: Ax = b ‚Üí PLUx = b ‚Üí Ly = Pb ‚Üí Ux = y

deepDive.heading: QR Decomposition
deepDive.body: A = QR: Q orthogonal (Q·µÄQ=I), R upper triangular. Methods: Gram-Schmidt (unstable), modified Gram-Schmidt, Householder reflections (stable), Givens rotations. Least squares: min||Ax-b||‚ÇÇ = min||Rx-Q·µÄb||‚ÇÇ. Solve Rx = Q·µÄb (upper triangular).

deepDive.heading: Singular Value Decomposition
deepDive.body: A = UŒ£V·µÄ. U, V orthogonal; Œ£ diagonal with singular values œÉ‚ÇÅ ‚â• ‚Ä¶ ‚â• œÉ·µ£ > 0. Rank-k approximation: A‚Çñ = U‚ÇñŒ£‚ÇñV‚Çñ·µÄ. Pseudo-inverse A‚Å∫ = VŒ£‚Å∫U·µÄ. Best rank-k approximation in spectral and Frobenius norm (Eckart-Young-Mirsky).
deepDive.formula: A = UŒ£V·µÄ, A‚Å∫ = V diag(1/œÉ‚ÇÅ,‚Ä¶,1/œÉ·µ£,0,‚Ä¶) U·µÄ

deepDive.heading: Eigendecomposition
deepDive.body: A = VDV‚Åª¬π for diagonalizable A. D = diag(Œª‚ÇÅ,‚Ä¶,Œª‚Çô). Powers: A‚Åø = VD‚ÅøV‚Åª¬π. Exponential: e^A = Ve^DV‚Åª¬π. Symmetric A = QŒõQ·µÄ (orthogonal Q, real Œõ). Not all matrices diagonalizable (Jordan form for defective matrices).
deepDive.formula: A = VDV‚Åª¬π, A‚Åø = VD‚ÅøV‚Åª¬π, e^A = Ve^DV‚Åª¬π

deepDive.heading: Iterative Methods
deepDive.body: For large sparse systems: Conjugate Gradient (CG) for SPD, O(n) per iteration, converges in ‚â§n steps (exact arithmetic). GMRES for general, BiCGSTAB for non-symmetric. Preconditioning crucial: solve M‚Åª¬πAx = M‚Åª¬πb with M ‚âà A cheap to invert.

takeaways: LU: solving systems. Cholesky: SPD, faster.
SVD: low-rank approximation, pseudo-inverse, PCA. Most versatile.
Large sparse: iterative methods (CG, GMRES) with preconditioning.

edgeCases.q: When should I use SVD instead of eigendecomposition?
edgeCases.a: (1) Non-square matrices (SVD always exists). (2) Numerical stability: computing A·µÄA squares condition number. (3) Rank-deficient matrices (SVD handles gracefully). (4) Need pseudo-inverse. Eigendecomposition is faster when A is symmetric and you know it's well-conditioned.

edgeCases.q: What happens if a matrix is not diagonalizable?
edgeCases.a: Use Jordan decomposition A = VJV‚Åª¬π where J is Jordan form (block diagonal with eigenvalues on diagonal, 1s on superdiagonal). Or use Schur decomposition A = UTU* which always exists. For numerical computation, Schur is preferred; Jordan is sensitive to perturbations.

edgeCases.q: When do iterative methods beat direct methods?
edgeCases.a: For large sparse matrices. Direct methods (LU, Cholesky) suffer fill-in ‚Äî sparse A becomes dense L and U. Iterative methods only need matrix-vector products, preserving sparsity. CG converges fast if condition number is small or eigenvalues are clustered. For 3D PDEs, iterative often win when n > 10‚Å¥.

sources.label: Golub & Van Loan ‚Äî Matrix Computations (4th ed.)
